title: Protecting What You're Building
description: Intellectual property, contracts, insurance, and knowing when to bring in a lawyer. How to protect your business assets in the U.S. legal system without overspending.

moduleLearningOutcomes: Distinguish between copyright, trademark, and patent protections and identify which apply to their business
Describe key contract, insurance, and regulatory compliance requirements
Assess when professional legal counsel is needed and estimate associated costs

lessons.title: Intellectual Property — Protecting Your Ideas in the U.S.
objectives: Distinguish between copyright, trademark, and patent protections in the U.S.
Identify which IP protections apply to their business assets
Estimate filing costs and timelines for trademark and patent applications

contentBlocks.title: Welcome to the Launch Facility, Protecting What You Have Built
contentBlocks.content:
A founder from Chile built a successful delivery app across South America. When she expanded to Florida, she discovered that her Chilean trademark registration meant nothing in the United States. Intellectual property rights are territorial — protection in one country does not automatically extend to another. If you plan to operate in the U.S., you need U.S. protection.

There are three main types of IP protection, each covering different aspects of your business. The comparison below explains what each one protects, what it costs, and when you need it.

rows: Creative works: code, content, designs, media
Automatic on creation; register for enforcement
Usually not needed for basic filing

items.label: Protects
items.value: Original creative works (code, text, designs, music)

items.label: Automatic?
items.value: Yes — exists at creation, registration strengthens it

items.label: Automatic?
items.value: No — must file with USPTO for federal protection

items.label: Lawyer Needed?
items.value: Yes — complex process requires patent attorney

items.label: Automatic?
items.value: No — must file and be approved by USPTO

contentBlocks.title: Trademark Registration Timeline — USPTO
contentBlocks.titleEs: Línea de Tiempo de Registro de Marca — USPTO

milestones.label: File Application
milestones.desc: TEAS Plus ($250/class) or TEAS Standard ($350/class). Include mark, goods/services description, specimen of use.
milestones.descEs: TEAS Plus ($250/clase) o TEAS Standard ($350/clase). Incluir marca, descripción de bienes/servicios, muestra de uso.

milestones.label: USPTO Review
milestones.desc: Examining attorney reviews for conflicts. Average wait: 3–4 months.
milestones.descEs: El abogado examinador revisa conflictos. Espera promedio: 3–4 meses.

milestones.label: Office Actions (if any)
milestones.desc: You may get a refusal or request for clarification. 3-month deadline to respond.
milestones.descEs: Puede recibir un rechazo o solicitud de aclaración. Plazo de 3 meses para responder.

milestones.label: Publication
milestones.desc: Mark published in the Official Gazette for 30 days. Anyone can oppose.
milestones.descEs: Marca publicada en la Gaceta Oficial por 30 días. Cualquiera puede oponerse.

milestones.label: Registration
milestones.desc: If no opposition: registration certificate issued. Total time: 8–12 months typical.
milestones.descEs: Sin oposición: se emite el certificado de registro. Tiempo total: 8–12 meses típico.

milestones.label: Maintain
milestones.desc: Declaration of Use at year 5–6 and renewal every 10 years.
milestones.descEs: Declaración de Uso en el año 5–6 y renovación cada 10 años.

contentBlocks.callout: International founders: Your home country trademark does NOT protect you in the U.S. IP rights are territorial — you must register separately.
contentBlocks.calloutEs: Fundadores internacionales: su marca registrada en su país NO lo protege en EE.UU. Los derechos de propiedad intelectual son territoriales — debe registrarse por separado.
contentBlocks.footer: Total cost: $250–$750 for filing + $0 if self-filed, $1,500–$3,000 with attorney.
contentBlocks.footerEs: Costo total: $250–$750 por presentación + $0 si lo hace solo, $1,500–$3,000 con abogado.

contentBlocks.title: Trademark Registration, What Actually Happens
contentBlocks.content:
A trademark is the emblem that distinguishes your business from everyone else's. The registration process through the USPTO is straightforward but takes patience.

Step 1: Search the USPTO database at trademark.uspto.gov. If a conflict exists, your application is rejected and your filing fee is not refunded.

Step 2: File your application online through TEAS. Choose the right class of goods or services (45 classes). Filing fee: $250 (TEAS Plus) or $350 (TEAS Standard) per class.

Step 3: Wait for examination. A USPTO examining attorney reviews your application in 3–4 months. They may issue an "office action" requesting changes; respond within the deadline or your application is abandoned.

Step 4: Publication period. Your trademark is published in the Official Gazette for 30 days. If no one opposes, you receive your registration certificate.

Total timeline: 8–12 months. A Polish cybersecurity founder hired a U.S. trademark attorney before opening her Orlando office. The $800 she spent was a fraction of the $15,000–$25,000 she would have paid to fight a conflict later.

contentBlocks.title: Reduce Risk — Register Your Trademark Before You Launch
contentBlocks.content: Why does this matter? If someone else files your brand name in the U.S. first, you may lose it — even if you've used it for years in another country. The U.S. trademark system gives strong rights to the first filer. Start the process before you invest in U.S. marketing, packaging, or signage. The $250–$350 filing fee is far less than the cost of rebranding.

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make
contentBlocks.content:
• Assuming home-country IP protection covers the U.S. IP protection is country-by-country. Your trademark filed in Brazil, Germany, Colombia, or anywhere else gives you zero coverage in the United States. Trademarks, patents, and enforceable copyright all require separate U.S. filings. If you wait, someone else can register your brand name in the U.S. first — and they will have the legal right to use it here. File your U.S. trademark application before you start marketing in the U.S., not after.
  • Running without general liability insurance. This isn't an IP issue, but it's a Module 3 pattern: founders focus on the legal setup and forget to guard against day-to-day business risks. One lawsuit without coverage can shut you down. (More on insurance in the next lesson.)
  • Using free contract templates without legal review. Free NDA templates and service agreements from legal websites are a starting point, not a finished product. They may not follow Florida law, may be missing key clauses, or may not fit your situation. A 30-minute attorney review costs $150-$300 and can prevent costly disputes later.

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money: What This Actually Costs
contentBlocks.content:
In other words, plan your IP budget early. IP, insurance, and legal costs you should budget for (as of early 2026):

  • Trademark filing (USPTO): $250–$350 per class, plus $300–$500 if using an attorney
  • Patent application: $5,000–$15,000+ including attorney fees and USPTO filing costs
  • Copyright registration: $45–$65 per work
  • NDA or contract drafting: $200–$500 per document
  • General liability insurance: $400–$800/year for small businesses
  • Professional liability (E&O): $500–$2,500/year depending on industry
  • Initial legal consultation: $0–$300 (many attorneys offer free first consultations)
  • Contract review by attorney: $200–$500 per document

questions.stem: A founder has been using a brand name in Colombia for three years and now wants to sell products under that name in the U.S. What should she do first?
options: Search the USPTO database and file a U.S. trademark application before launching
Start selling immediately. Her Colombian trademark automatically applies in the U.S.
Wait until she has U.S. revenue, then file for a trademark
Use the TM symbol and skip formal registration since she has prior use
questions.explanation: IP protection is territorial. A Colombian trademark has no legal effect in the U.S. The founder should search the USPTO database for conflicts and file a U.S. trademark application before investing in U.S. marketing. Waiting increases the risk that someone else files the same name first.

questions.stem: Which type of IP protection is most commonly needed first by international founders entering the U.S. market?
options: Patent: to protect their business method
Copyright: to protect their website content
Trademark: to protect their brand name and logo
Trade secret: to protect proprietary processes
questions.explanation: Most international founders need trademark protection first because it protects the brand name and logo they'll use in the U.S. market. Copyright exists automatically (though registration helps enforcement), and patents are only needed for genuine inventions. Trademark is the highest-priority IP filing for most new market entrants.

questions.stem: A founder from Ghana has developed a unique mobile payment algorithm and wants to protect both the technology and the brand name before entering the U.S. market. She has $10,000 budgeted for IP filings. Based on typical costs and timelines, which approach best uses her budget?
options: File both a patent and a trademark simultaneously to save time, since both go through the USPTO
Skip formal IP filings and rely on the automatic copyright protection her code already has
File a patent first since it offers the broadest protection and covers her brand name as well
File for copyright registration on her code and pursue trademark registration now, then budget separately for a patent application later since patents cost $5,000–$15,000 and take 2–3 years
questions.explanation: With $10,000, filing both a trademark ($250–$850 with attorney) and copyright ($45–$65) fits the budget while protecting the brand name and code. A patent application ($5,000–$15,000+) would consume most or all of the budget and takes 2–3 years to process. Filing the trademark and copyright now — then budgeting separately for a patent — gives her immediate protection where it matters most. Patents do not cover brand names, so option C is incorrect. Relying only on automatic copyright (option B) leaves the brand name unprotected. Filing both a patent and trademark simultaneously (option A) would exceed the $10,000 budget.

contentBlocks.title: Reflection: Your IP Inventory
contentBlocks.prompt: List every intellectual property asset your company has: brand name, logo, product names, proprietary software, content, inventions, or processes. For each one, identify which U.S. protection type applies (copyright, trademark, or patent) and whether you've already filed or need to.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: Apply this lesson before you invest in U.S. marketing: trademark your brand name first. Evaluate which IP protections your business needs, then create a filing plan. Start with a USPTO trademark search, file early, and budget $250–$850 for the trademark process. For patents, hire an attorney. For copyright, register before you need to enforce it. Your intellectual property is only as safe as the protections you put around it — act before someone else does.

lessons.title: Insurance and Risk Management
objectives: Describe basic business insurance requirements and coverage options for U.S. operations
Estimate insurance costs and identify which policies are needed before launch
Explain key regulatory exposure areas for international founders

contentBlocks.title: Shielding Your Business: Insurance and Risk Management
contentBlocks.content:
Now that you have locked down your intellectual property inside the launch facility, walk over to the safety and compliance wing. No business should operate without proper insurance and risk management.

A founder from Costa Rica lost $22,000 because he closed a deal with a handshake instead of a contract. In the U.S., that handshake is worth almost nothing — three months later, the distributor changed payment terms and refused to pay outstanding invoices. Without a signed contract, the founder had no legal leverage. This is why contracts matter (we cover contract types in the next lesson), but first, you need to understand the insurance and risk management foundations that protect your business every day.

Many international founders focus on the legal setup — entity formation, IP, contracts — and forget to guard against day-to-day business risks. One lawsuit without insurance coverage can shut you down. One slip-and-fall at your office, one product defect, one professional error that costs a client money. Insurance is your safety net for the risks you cannot predict.

contentBlocks.title: Business Insurance — What Coverage Do You Need?

cards.heading: General Liability
cards.desc: Required by most: Covers customer injuries, property damage, lawsuits. Get this FIRST.
cards.descEs: Requerido por la mayoría: Cubre lesiones a clientes, daños a propiedad, demandas. Obténlo PRIMERO.

cards.heading: Professional Liability / E&O
cards.desc: For service businesses: Covers mistakes, negligence claims, missed deadlines. Required if you give advice or provide professional services.
cards.descEs: Para negocios de servicios: Cubre errores, reclamos por negligencia, plazos incumplidos. Requerido si das asesoría o servicios profesionales.

cards.heading: Workers' Compensation
cards.desc: Required in Florida (4+ employees): Covers employee injuries on the job. Cost varies by industry and payroll. Mandatory — not optional.
cards.descEs: Requerido en Florida (4+ empleados): Cubre lesiones laborales. El costo varía por industria y nómina. Obligatorio — no opcional.

cards.heading: Commercial Property
cards.desc: If you have a physical space: Covers equipment, inventory, office contents. Includes fire, theft, some natural disasters.
cards.descEs: Si tienes un espacio físico: Cubre equipo, inventario, contenido de oficina. Incluye incendio, robo, algunos desastres naturales.

cards.heading: Business Owner's Policy / BOP
cards.desc: Best value for small businesses: Bundles general liability + property insurance at a discount. Great starting point.
cards.descEs: Mejor valor para pequeños negocios: Combina responsabilidad general + seguro de propiedad con descuento. Excelente punto de partida.

cards.heading: Cyber Liability
cards.desc: For any business handling customer data: Covers data breaches, ransomware, notification costs. Increasingly essential.
cards.descEs: Para cualquier negocio que maneje datos de clientes: Cubre violaciones de datos, ransomware, costos de notificación. Cada vez más esencial.

contentBlocks.footer: How to Buy: Get quotes from 3+ providers, use an independent insurance broker, review annually. Just starting? Get General Liability first. Adding employees? Add Workers' Comp. Handling data? Add Cyber Liability.
contentBlocks.footerEs: Cómo Comprar: Obtén cotizaciones de 3+ proveedores, usa un corredor de seguros independiente, revisa anualmente. ¿Recién empezando? Obtén Responsabilidad General primero. ¿Contratando empleados? Agrega Compensación al Trabajador. ¿Manejando datos? Agrega Responsabilidad Cibernética.

contentBlocks.title: Did You Know?
contentBlocks.content: Central Florida's Space Coast has hosted over 4,000 rocket launches since 1950, including every crewed NASA mission. The same culture of risk management that keeps rockets safe also drives the region's insurance industry: Florida has one of the largest property insurance markets in the country. For business founders, the parallel is clear — just as every launch requires a safety review, every business needs protection against the risks that could ground your operation.

contentBlocks.title: Business Insurance, What You Need and What Can Wait
contentBlocks.content:
Insurance in the U.S. is not optional. Some types are required by law. Others are required in practice, since clients and landlords demand proof.

General Liability Insurance (GL): Your most important policy. It covers third-party bodily injury, property damage, and advertising injury. Cost: $400–$800/year. Many landlords and B2B clients require proof before they will work with you.

Professional Liability / E&O: Covers claims your professional services caused financial harm — a software bug, bad advice, or a missed deadline. Cost: $500–$2,500/year.

Workers' Compensation: Required in Florida with four or more employees (one in construction). Cost varies by industry and payroll.

Commercial Property Insurance: Covers equipment, inventory, and office contents. Skip if working from home; get it for a physical office or warehouse.

A Nigerian founder found that U.S. vendor onboarding required proof of $1 million GL and $1 million E&O before clients would sign. Budget for insurance before you need it.

contentBlocks.title: Operating Without General Liability Insurance
contentBlocks.content: Here's where the risk is real. One slip-and-fall, one product issue, one bad contract — and you're personally exposed if your business doesn't have GL coverage. Even if your LLC provides liability protection, courts can "pierce the corporate veil" if you're operating without basic insurance. GL is affordable ($400–$800/year for most small businesses) and many landlords and B2B clients require it. Don't skip it.

contentBlocks.title: Insurance Types — What Each Policy Covers

sections.heading: General Liability Insurance
sections.content:
**Covers:** Third-party bodily injury, property damage, advertising injury.

**Cost:** $400–$1,500/year for small businesses.

**Who needs it:** Almost every business. Often required by landlords and contracts.

sections.heading: Professional Liability (E&O)
sections.content:
**Covers:** Claims of negligence, errors, or failure to deliver professional services.

**Cost:** $500–$3,000/year depending on industry.

**Who needs it:** Consultants, IT companies, any service-based business.

sections.heading: Workers' Compensation
sections.content:
**Covers:** Employee injuries and illnesses on the job.

**Cost:** Varies by industry and payroll size.

**Who needs it:** Required in Florida if you have 4+ employees (construction: 1+ employees).

sections.heading: Cyber Liability Insurance
sections.content:
**Covers:** Data breaches, ransomware, notification costs, legal defense.

**Cost:** $500–$5,000/year for small businesses.

**Who needs it:** Any business that stores customer data, processes payments, or relies on technology.

contentBlocks.title: Regulatory Exposure, Industries That Need Extra Attention
contentBlocks.content:
Some industries have additional rules you must follow before operating in the U.S. Regulatory agencies will not clear your business without the right permits, and the consequences of non-compliance are severe. Central Florida's economy spans aerospace, tourism, healthcare, technology, and agriculture, and each sector brings its own regulatory requirements.

Food and Beverage: FDA (federal) and FDACS (Florida) compliance — labeling, inspections, product testing. A Colombian coffee brand spent $8,000 on FDA-compliant labeling before listing on Amazon.

Healthcare and Wellness: HIPAA compliance for protected health information. Medical devices need FDA clearance. Telehealth faces state-by-state licensing.

Financial Services: SEC, FINRA, and state-level regulations. Money transmission licenses cost $10,000–$50,000+ across multiple states. A Nigerian founder scaled back from 50 states to five key markets, cutting first-year compliance from $100,000+ to roughly $25,000.

Children's Products: CPSC testing and certification — lead, small parts, labeling. Third-party testing costs $1,000–$5,000 per product line.

Penalties for operating without permits include fines, product seizures, and shutdowns. The SBDC and UCF BIP advisors can help identify which regulations are relevant to your business.

rows: Before signing a commercial lease or B2B contracts
Practically required — most clients and landlords demand it
Before providing professional services to U.S. clients
Required by many B2B clients in service industries
When you hire your 4th Florida employee (1st in construction)
Legally required above the employee threshold
When you have a physical office, warehouse, or inventory
Required if you have a commercial lease
If you handle customer data or process payments

questions.stem: A founder selling consulting services to U.S. companies is asked to provide proof of insurance during vendor onboarding. Which two types of insurance are most likely required?
options: Workers' compensation and commercial property insurance
General liability and professional liability (E&O)
Cyber liability and directors & officers (D&O) insurance
Health insurance and disability insurance
questions.explanation: U.S. companies commonly require vendors to carry general liability (for physical and property risks) and professional liability/E&O (for financial harm from professional services). These are the two most frequently requested insurance types during B2B vendor onboarding.

questions.stem: A founder plans to sell packaged food products in the U.S. through Amazon. She has already registered her LLC and obtained general liability insurance. What regulatory step is she most likely missing?
options: Obtaining FDA-compliant labeling and meeting FDACS requirements before listing products
Filing a trademark for her brand name with the USPTO
Purchasing cyber liability insurance to protect customer payment data
Registering for workers' compensation insurance in Florida
questions.explanation: Food and beverage businesses must comply with FDA (federal) and FDACS (Florida) regulations, including labeling requirements, facility inspections, and potentially product testing. These regulatory requirements must be met before selling food products in the U.S., regardless of the sales channel. Filing a trademark (option B) is important but not a regulatory requirement for food sales. Cyber liability insurance (option C) and workers' comp (option D) are not the most likely missing step for a food product launch.

questions.stem: A founder from Vietnam is preparing to sign her first client contract with a U.S. marketing agency. The agency wants to discuss her proprietary analytics methodology before signing. She has no written agreements in place yet. Which contract should she ask the agency to sign first?
options: A service agreement covering scope, payment terms, and deliverables for the project
A terms-of-service document for her company website
An operating agreement for her LLC defining member responsibilities
A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) to protect her proprietary methodology before sharing details
questions.explanation: Before sharing proprietary information, the founder should have the agency sign an NDA. This protects her analytics methodology during the discussion phase. A service agreement (option A) comes after both parties agree on terms. Terms of service (option B) is for her website users, not a client negotiation. An operating agreement (option C) is an internal LLC document, not relevant to a client relationship.

contentBlocks.title: Insurance Portfolio Builder
contentBlocks.description: Now build YOUR insurance stack. Select your business profile and see which policies you need.
contentBlocks.descriptionEs: Ahora arme SU paquete de seguros. Seleccione su perfil de negocio y vea qué pólizas necesita.

inputs.label: Business Type
options: Professional Services
Technology / SaaS
Retail
Food & Beverage
Construction
General / Other

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The takeaway is clear: get your contracts in writing — every time, with every partner. Carry general liability insurance from day one ($400–$800/year). If you provide professional services, add E&O coverage. Check whether your industry has specific regulatory requirements before you start operating. Apply these steps early: the costs are far cheaper than lawsuits, fines, or lost contracts. Evaluate your coverage needs now, before a client or landlord asks for proof.

lessons.title: Contracts and When You Need a Lawyer
objectives: Identify situations that require professional legal counsel vs. what you can handle yourself
Estimate legal costs for common business needs (entity formation, contracts, trademark, disputes)
Describe how to find and evaluate a business attorney for international founders

contentBlocks.title: DIY or Call an Expert?
contentBlocks.content:
You have secured your intellectual property and your insurance is in place. Now comes the last major question for this module: which decisions can you make on your own, and which ones require a specialist?

A business attorney in Central Florida charges $200 to $400 per hour. Call one for every question and you will burn through your budget fast. A founder from Ecuador saved $3,000 by filing his own LLC and EIN, then spent $800 on an attorney for the operating agreement and a major client contract. The comparison below shows where you can save and where you should invest.

columns.heading: Handle It Yourself
items: Basic NDA using a reputable template
Sales tax registration with Florida DOR (free)

columns.heading: Get a Lawyer
items: Any situation involving threatened legal action

rows: Many attorneys offer a free 30-minute consultation. Always ask.
Flat fee is common for simple contracts. Complex agreements may be hourly.
Service agreements, partnership agreements, licensing deals.
Includes entity selection advice, filing, and operating agreement drafting.
Attorney fee on top of USPTO filing fee ($250–$350).
When your business structure affects your visa pathway. Budget at the higher end.
Pre-litigation work. If it goes to court, costs escalate significantly.
Business attorneys. Immigration specialists may be higher.

contentBlocks.title: Before You Hire a Lawyer
contentBlocks.content:
Think about this before you spend money on legal fees: exhaust the free resources available to you first.

  • SBDC (Small Business Development Center): Free one-on-one business counseling, including referrals to attorneys. The UCF SBDC is located on campus and serves the entire Central Florida region.
  • UCF BIP network: Ask your program advisors for attorney referrals — they work with lawyers who understand international founders.
  • Florida Bar Lawyer Referral Service: Connects you with attorneys by specialty for an initial consultation (typically $25–$50 for 30 minutes).
  • Legal Aid societies: If your budget is extremely tight, some legal aid organizations provide free or reduced-cost services to entrepreneurs.

When you do hire a lawyer, ask for flat-fee quotes instead of hourly billing. Verify they have global client experience. And always get the fee agreement in writing before work begins. Central Florida's technology and aerospace sectors have created a deep pool of attorneys experienced in international business, IP, and immigration, so finding the right specialist is more achievable here than in many other U.S. markets.

contentBlocks.title: When Do You Need a Lawyer? — Decision Timeline

steps.label: Formation
steps.title: Entity Setup
steps.content: For simple LLCs, you can DIY through SunBiz. For C-Corps seeking investors, an attorney ensures proper bylaws and stock structure.

steps.label: First Contract
steps.title: Contract Review
steps.content: Have an attorney review your first client or vendor contract. Once you have a solid template, you can reuse it.

steps.label: First Hire
steps.title: Employment Law
steps.content: Contractor vs. employee classification has serious legal consequences. Get advice before your first hire.

steps.label: IP Filing
steps.title: Trademark or Patent
steps.content: Trademarks can be filed solo, but patents require a patent attorney. Budget $5K–$15K for patent work.

steps.label: Dispute
steps.title: Conflict Resolution
steps.content: If you receive a legal threat, demand letter, or lawsuit, engage an attorney immediately. Don't respond on your own.

contentBlocks.title: Finding the Right Attorney, They Are Not All the Same
contentBlocks.content:
"Lawyer" is as broad as "doctor." You would not see a cardiologist for a broken arm, and the same principle applies to legal counsel. Different situations require different specialists.

Business attorney: Entity formation, contracts, commercial disputes. Your primary legal relationship.

Immigration attorney: Visas, work authorization, immigration-linked business decisions. Essential if your business structure affects your ability to stay in the U.S. (common for E-2 or L-1 holders).

IP attorney: Trademarks, patents, copyright enforcement. A general business attorney is not enough for patent filings or trademark conflicts.

Tax attorney: International tax treaties, IRS audits, multi-country income. Use a CPA for routine work; a tax attorney for disputes involving $50,000+ liability.

Questions to ask before hiring:

  1. Do you work with international business owners?
  2. Are you familiar with E-2 / O-1 / L-1 visa implications?
  3. Do you offer flat-fee billing?
  4. Can you provide references from international founders?

A Mexican founder interviewed three attorneys — the first two had no experience with foreign-owned businesses; the third had helped 15 international founders and offered $1,200 flat-fee for entity formation and operating agreement.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Legal
contentBlocks.content:
Interestingly, Central Florida has a strong network of bilingual attorneys who specialize in international business:

  • Hispanic Bar Association of Central Florida: Attorney referrals by specialty, networking events, and pro bono programs
  • Prospera: Offers legal workshops for Hispanic entrepreneurs, including business formation and contract basics
  • UCF BIP network: Access to vetted attorneys who regularly work with international founders from Latin America

Having an attorney who speaks your language and understands your cultural context can make a significant difference, especially for immigration-linked business decisions.

questions.stem: Which of the following situations does NOT typically require a lawyer?
options: Filing an LLC formation document on SunBiz
Negotiating an investor equity agreement
Responding to a trademark infringement cease-and-desist letter
Structuring a business to support an E-2 visa application
questions.explanation: SunBiz LLC filing is straightforward and can be completed online for $125 without legal assistance. Investor agreements, IP disputes, and immigration-linked business structuring all involve significant legal complexity and financial risk — these situations warrant professional legal counsel.

questions.stem: A founder needs to structure her U.S. business in a way that supports her E-2 visa application. She already has a business attorney who handled her LLC formation. What should she do?
options: Ask her business attorney to handle the visa-related structuring since they already know her company
Skip the attorney and structure the business herself using online guides
Consult an immigration attorney for the visa-linked decisions, since this requires specialized expertise beyond general business law
Wait until her visa is approved before making any business structure decisions
questions.explanation: Immigration-linked business structuring requires an immigration attorney, not a general business attorney. Just as you would not go to a cardiologist for a broken arm, visa-related decisions need a specialist who understands how business structure affects immigration status. The business attorney handles corporate matters; the immigration attorney handles visa-specific requirements.

questions.stem: A founder from Indonesia is planning her first year of U.S. operations. She needs an LLC formation with operating agreement, one client service contract reviewed, and a trademark filing. Using the cost ranges from this lesson, what is the most realistic total legal budget she should set aside?
options: Under $500 — she can handle everything herself using free online tools
$1,200–$3,000 — covering attorney-assisted entity formation ($500–$1,500), contract review ($200–$500), and trademark filing with attorney ($500–$1,000 plus USPTO fees)
$10,000–$20,000 — U.S. legal services are extremely expensive for all tasks
$5,000–$8,000 — she needs a full-time attorney on retainer for the first year
questions.explanation: Based on the cost ranges in this lesson: entity formation with attorney runs $500–$1,500, contract review is $200–$500 per document, and trademark filing with attorney is $500–$1,000 plus the $250–$350 USPTO fee. The total falls in the $1,200–$3,000 range. Option A underestimates the cost of attorney involvement. Options C and D significantly overestimate the costs for these specific services.

contentBlocks.title: Reflection: Your Legal Needs
contentBlocks.prompt: Think about your first 6 months of U.S. operations. What legal tasks can you handle yourself? Where will you need professional help? Write down your top 3 legal priorities and estimate the total cost using the ranges from this lesson. Have you identified an attorney yet, or do you need to start that search?

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The takeaway is clear. Use free resources first: SBDC counseling, UCF BIP referrals, and Florida Bar referral services. Handle simple filings yourself (SunBiz, EIN, sales tax registration). Save your attorney budget for the decisions that carry real risk — investor agreements, IP protection, immigration-linked structuring, and contracts worth serious money. Apply this principle: ask for flat fees, verify international experience, and always get the fee agreement in writing. Evaluate each legal need against the cost tables above before you commit. Make sure the right specialists are in the room before you make decisions that affect your business long-term.
